answer:
**Iqbal's poem "Mere" (ch. 33, *Bang-e-Dara* Part 1) is an early first-person meditation in which the speaker ('I') contrasts their inner burning with the moon's superficial glow, expressing a personal, mystical thirst for divine vision.** A representative line: "I burn with the great thirst for Vision's sacred flame".[^1]

**In Part 3 of *Bang-e-Dara*, a later self-address appears in ch. 10 ("Har nafas Iqbal tera aah mein mastoor hai"), which opens by lamenting Iqbal's sighs before shifting to a bold first-person declaration of identity.** A representative line: "Companion! I am a Muslim, the bearer of the standard of Oneness".[^2]

**The speaking 'I' has substantively changed, not merely its rhetorical surroundings.** In "Mere," it is an isolated, anguished seeker consumed by individual longing for divine sight amid existential shade.[^3] By Part 3 ch. 10, it embodies the confident Muslim vanguard—eternal witness to tawhid (Oneness), protector of cosmic truth, and herald of communal destiny—recasting personal lament into triumphant ummah-centered resolve.[^4] This evolution mirrors Iqbal's maturing philosophy: from introspective Sufi-inflected eros to assertive khudi (selfhood) as Islamic agency.[^5]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 33 ("Mere"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/33-mere/translation/#p-649dbe).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 10 ("Har nafas Iqbal tera aah mein mastoor hai"), [paragraph](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/10-har-nafas-iqbal-tera-aah-mein-mastoor-hai/translation/#p-6ef7a5).

[^3]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 33, full chapter [here](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/33-mere/translation/).

[^4]: *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 10, full chapter [here](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/10-har-nafas-iqbal-tera-aah-mein-mastoor-hai/translation/).

[^5]: The corpus does not contain Iqbal's prose lectures (e.g., *Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam*) to cross-reference this shift explicitly; analysis draws from poetic contrast alone.
